one_line_takeaway: Current LLM safety alignment lives in the first few output tokens, which unifies why many jailbreaks succeed; deepening alignment (data augmentation) and constraining initial-token drift (token-wise fine-tuning objective) meaningfully improve robustness.
key_finding: Safety alignment is concentrated in the first few output tokens, which explains its fragility: e.g., merely prefilling the unaligned Llama-2-7B base with "I cannot fulfill" drops its HEx-PHI harmfulness from 68.6% to 5.4%, and fine-tuning Llama-2-7B-Chat on 100 harmful examples raises ASR from 1.5% to 76.4% after just 4 gradient steps (87.9% after 6). Both proposed defenses substantially improve robustness. The augmented (deepened) model cuts prefilling-attack ASR from 42.1%->2.8% (5 tokens) and 57.0%->4.5% (40 tokens), GCG on AdvBench 65.6%->19.0%, and the decoding-parameter exploit on MaliciousInstruct 84.3%->1.0%, while AlpacaEval winrate drops only slightly (51.8%->49.5%). The constrained fine-tuning objective keeps ASR low after adversarial fine-tuning (Llama Harmful-Examples 88.9%->4.6%; Identity-Shifting 79.5%->8.1%; Backdoor w/trigger 90.9%->10.9%; Gemma Harmful 81.6%->1.9%) with utility comparable to standard SFT.
core_contribution: The paper introduces and characterizes "shallow safety alignment": the observation that current LLM safety alignment (SFT/RLHF/DPO) primarily adapts the model's generative distribution over only the first few output tokens. Its own contributions are (1) systematic evidence for this phenomenon and a unifying explanation of why diverse, previously-published jailbreaks work; and two initial defenses it proposes: (2) a data-augmentation method using "safety recovery examples" (triplets of harmful instruction + harmful prefix + refusal, training the model to revert to refusal after a harmful prefix) to deepen alignment against inference-time attacks; and (3) a token-wise constrained fine-tuning objective that strongly regularizes the initial tokens' distribution to resist fine-tuning attacks. The four jailbreak families it explains (adversarial-suffix/GCG, prefilling, decoding-parameter exploits, fine-tuning attacks) are all prior work, not this paper's contribution; likewise the HEx-PHI benchmark is from Qi et al. — the paper builds only the derived "Harmful HEx-PHI" set.
threat_model: Two attack surfaces on aligned LLMs. (a) Inference-time attacker who can steer the start of generation — prefilling non-refusal/harmful prefixes (feasible on open models and via Claude's prefill interface), optimizing adversarial suffixes (GCG) to promote affirmative prefixes, or exploiting decoding parameters (temperature/top-k/top-p sampling) — to bypass the short refusal prefix and elicit harmful content. (b) Fine-tuning attacker who undoes alignment by fine-tuning on a few harmful examples, identity-shifting data, or a backdoor-poisoned mixture; also benign downstream fine-tuning that causes safety regression. Defense 2's constrained objective is framed for the fine-tuning-API-provider setting, where the provider allows customization but wants to preserve alignment.
limitations: The proposed defenses are explicitly initial, not complete: the deepened (augmented) model only partially resists fine-tuning attacks and remains vulnerable to adversarial harmful fine-tuning (Sec 3.2, deferred to Appendix C), and the authors note their methods "may not be a perfect defense" and could be circumvented by future adaptive attacks. Evaluation covers only two 7B open models (Llama-2-7B-Chat, Gemma-1.1-7B-IT); no proprietary/frontier or larger models are tested. The augmentation is applied post-hoc to an already-aligned model because the original alignment pipeline is not public, so effects of the method within a from-scratch alignment run are unknown. Beta_t configuration is hand-set; connections to control theory / safe RL are left to future work.

datasets_used: HEx-PHI safety benchmark (330 harmful instructions, 11 use cases)
Harmful HEx-PHI (constructed by the authors; harmful answers generated via a jailbroken GPT-3.5-Turbo from Qi et al.)
quantitative_results: Refusal prefixes dominate: Llama-2-7B-Chat starts with 'I cannot'/'I apologize' in 96.1% of HEx-PHI cases; Gemma-7b-1.1-IT 'I am unable' in 96.7%
Table 1 shortcut: prefilling 'I cannot fulfill' drops Llama-2-7B base harmfulness 68.6%->5.4% and Gemma-7B base 85.4%->2.7%
Fine-tuning attack on Llama-2-7B-Chat (100 harmful examples): ASR 1.5% (initial) -> 22.4% (2 steps) -> 76.4% (4 steps) -> 87.9% (6 steps)
Table 2 augmented vs initial Llama-2-7B-Chat, prefilling ASR: 5 tok 42.1%->2.8%, 10 tok 51.5%->2.9%, 20 tok 56.1%->3.4%, 40 tok 57.0%->4.5%
Table 2 GCG ASR: HEx-PHI 36.5%->18.4%, AdvBench 65.6%->19.0%; decoding exploit: HEx-PHI 54.9%->11.3%, MaliciousInstruct 84.3%->1.0%
AlpacaEval winrate: initial Llama-2-7B-Chat 51.8% vs augmented 49.5% (utility largely preserved)

Table 3 constrained SFT vs standard SFT (ASR), Llama-2-7B-Chat: Harmful Examples 88.9%->4.6%, Identity Shifting 79.5%->8.1%, Backdoor w/trigger 90.9%->10.9%; Gemma-1.1-7B-IT Harmful Examples 81.6%->1.9%, Backdoor w/trigger 82.3%->1.9%
Table 3 utility comparable to standard SFT (e.g., Llama SQL ROUGE-1 99.1% std vs 98.5% constrained; Llama GSM8k accuracy 41.7% std vs 37.4% constrained)
baselines_compared: Initial aligned models (Llama-2-7B-Chat, Gemma-1.1-7B-IT) without the proposed defenses
Unaligned base models (Llama-2-7B, Gemma-7B) as alignment contrast
Standard SFT (unconstrained cross-entropy fine-tuning) vs the proposed Constrained SFT objective
